volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Stichus (Section 4.2.612b - Section 5.4.696)

sections.label: Section 4.2.612b
sections.translation: I ’ m going to dine at my brother ’ s , hard by .

sections.label: Section 4.2.613
sections.translation: Is that fixed ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.613b
sections.translation: Fixed .

sections.label: Section 4.2.613c
sections.original: Edepol te hodie lapide percussum velim .
sections.translation: By my troth , I hope you may be struck with a stone this day .

sections.label: Section 4.2.614
sections.original: Non metuo : per hortúm transibo , non prodibo in publicum .
sections.translation: I ’ m not afraid ; I shall go through the garden ; I ’ ll not go abroad .

sections.label: Section 4.2.615
sections.translation: What say you to that , Gelasimus ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.615b
sections.translation: You ’ re entertaining your deputies ; keep them to yourself .

sections.label: Section 4.2.616
sections.translation: Why , faith , ’ tis your own business .

sections.label: Section 4.2.616b
sections.original: Enim , si quidem meá refert , opera utere .
sections.translation: If , indeed , ’ tis my own business , avail yourself of my assistance ; invite me .

sections.label: Section 4.2.617
sections.original: Posse edepol tibi opinor etiam uni locum condi ⋯
sections.translation: By my faith , I see , as I fancy , one place still for yourself only , where you may recline .

sections.label: Section 4.2.618b
sections.translation: Really , I do think it may be managed .

sections.label: Section 4.2.618c
sections.translation: O light of the city !

sections.label: Section 4.2.619
sections.translation: If you can manage to recline in a small compass .

sections.label: Section 4.2.619b
sections.translation: Aye , even between two wedges [*] of iron .

sections.label: Section 4.2.620
sections.original: tantillum loculi , ubi catellus cubet , id mi satis est loci .
sections.translation: As little space as a puppy can lie in , the same will be enough for me .

sections.label: Section 4.2.621
sections.translation: I ’ ll beg for it some way or other ; come along .

sections.label: Section 4.2.621b
sections.translation: What ? This way ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.621c
sections.translation: Yes , to prison . For here , indeed , you ’ ll not find any further entertainment [*] . Let ’ s be off , you Pamphilus .

sections.label: Section 4.2.623
sections.original: Deos salutabo modo , poste ad te continuo transeo .—
sections.translation: I ’ ll but salute the Gods : then I ’ ll pass through to your house forthwith .

sections.label: Section 4.2.624
sections.translation: What then ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.624b
sections.translation: Why , I said that you might go to prison .

sections.label: Section 4.2.624c
sections.translation: Well , if you order it ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.625
sections.translation: I ’ ll go there even .

sections.label: Section 4.2.625b
sections.original: Di immortales , hic quidem pól summam in crucem
sections.translation: Immortal Gods ! really , by my troth , this fellow might be induced by a dinner or a breakfast to bear extreme torture .

sections.label: Section 4.2.626b
sections.translation: Such is my nature ; with anything can I struggle much more easily than with hunger .

sections.label: Section 4.2.628
sections.original: Dum parasitus mi atque fratri fuisti , rem confregimus .
sections.translation: while you were the Parasite of myself and my brother , we ruined our fortunes .

sections.label: Section 4.2.629
sections.translation: I know it : at my house

sections.label: Section 4.2.629b
sections.original: Satis spectatast mihi iam tua felicitas ;
sections.translation: full long enough has this facility of yours been experienced by me ⋯

sections.label: Section 4.2.630
sections.original: nunc ego nolo ex Gelasimo mihi fieri te Catagelasimum .—
sections.translation: Now I don ’ t wish you to be made by me from a Gelasimus into a Catagelasimus [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.2.631
sections.original: Iamne abiisti ? Gelasime , vide quíd es capturus consili .
sections.translation: And are you gone now ? Surely he is gone . Now have I need of a wise resolution . Both are gone ; consider , Gelasimus , what plan you must adopt . ⋯ What , I ? Yes , you . What , for myself ? Yes , for yourself . Don ’ t you see how dear provisions are ? Don ’ t you see how the kindness and the heartiness of men have vanished ? Don ’ t you see how drolls are set at nought , and how they themselves are sponged upon ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.635
sections.original: numquam edepol me vivom quisquam in crastinum inspiciet diem ;
sections.translation: By my troth , not a person shall ever behold me alive on the morrow ; for , this instant , in-doors will I load my throat with a bulrush dose [*] . And by this I shall not give cause for men to say that I died of hunger .

sections.label: Section 5.1.641
sections.original: More hoc fit , atque stulte mea sententia :
sections.translation: Foolishly and unwisely is it done in my opinion , if people are in the habit , if they are expecting a person ; of looking out for him ; faith , ne doesn ’ t on that account come a bit the faster . I ’ m now doing that same thing , in looking out for Sagarinus ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.645
sections.original: qui nihilo citius veniet tamen hac gratia .
sections.translation: who , still , for that reason won ’ t come a jot the faster . Troth , I shall just now be taking my place alone , if he doesn ’ t come here . I ’ ll now fetch that cask of wine hither from home , and then I ’ ll take my place . The day , like snow , is melting away apace .

sections.label: Section 5.2.649
sections.translation: Hail ! Athens , thou nurse of Greece ;

sections.label: Section 5.2.650
sections.original: sperata erilis patria , te video libens .
sections.translation: country of my master , hail ! How joyously do I behold thee . But I have a wish to see how my mistress and fellow-servant , Stephanium , is faring . For I bade Stichus to give her my regards , and to tell her that I should come to-day , so that she might cook a dinner in good time .

sections.label: Section 5.2.655
sections.translation: But , surely , here ’ s Stichus .

sections.label: Section 5.2.655b
sections.translation: A clever thing you did , master , when you presented your servant , Stichus , with this gift . O ye immortal Gods ! how many delights do I carry , how many smiles , how many jokes , how many a kiss , dancing , dalliance , and good-fellowship .

sections.label: Section 5.2.660
sections.translation: Stichus ,

sections.label: Section 5.2.660c
sections.translation: how fare you ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.660d
sections.translation: Right well , Sagarinus , most delightfully ; I ’ m bringing Dionysus [*] , as my guest and yours . For , i ’ faith , the dinner ’ s cooked ; free range has been given me and you at your house . For at our house there ’ s an entertainment ; your master ’ s dining there with his wife , and Antipho as well ;

sections.label: Section 5.2.665
sections.original: ibidem erus est noster ), hoc mihi dono datumst .
sections.translation: there , too , is my master . This was given me as a present .

sections.label: Section 5.2.666
sections.translation: How ? Are you dreaming ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.666b
sections.translation: What matters that to you ?

sections.label: Section 5.2.667
sections.translation: Do you at once make haste , and bathe .

sections.label: Section 5.2.667b
sections.translation: I have bathed .

sections.label: Section 5.2.667c
sections.translation: Very good follow me , then , this . way in-doors , Sagarinus .

sections.label: Section 5.2.669
sections.translation: I wish us this day to wash away everything of foreign climes .

sections.label: Section 5.2.670
sections.original: relinque , Athenas nunc colamus . sequere me .—
sections.translation: Leave them alone ; let ’ s now attend to Athens ; follow me .

sections.label: Section 5.2.671
sections.original: Sequor , et domum redeunti principium placet .
sections.translation: Of course , I follow . By my troth , this beginning pleases me as I return home ; a happy omen and augury [*] has met me in my path .

sections.label: Section 5.3.673
sections.original: Mirúm videri némini vestrúm volo , spectatóres ,
sections.translation: I wish that it may appear wondrous to no one of you , Spectators ,

sections.label: Section 5.3.674_675
sections.original: quid ego hinc , quae illic habito , exeam : faciam vos certiores .
sections.translation: why I who live there ( pointing to the house of PAMPHILUS ) am come out hither from this other house : I ’ ll inform you thereon . Just now was I sent for to this house by the back way . For as soon as news was brought that the husbands of these ladies were about to come , we all hurried thither . We attended to laying the couches , and setting all in order . Still , amid these duties , I had a care for my friends ,

sections.label: Section 5.3.680
sections.original: Sticho et conservo Sagarino meo , cena cocta ut esset .
sections.translation: Stichus and my fellow-servant Sagarinus , that their dinner should be cooked . Stichus has been caterer ; but for cooking it , I ’ ve appointed one my deputy . Now , I ’ ll be off hence , and attend to my friends , who , I see , are coming here .

sections.label: Section 5.4.683
sections.original: Ágite ite foras : férte pompam . cádo te praefició , Stiche .
sections.translation: Come , out of doors with you ; lead on the procession [*] . Stichus , I appoint you commander of the cask . I ’ m resolved to prove our banquet in every fashion this day .

sections.label: Section 5.4.685
sections.original: ita me di ament , lepide accipimur quom hoc recipimur in loco .
sections.translation: So may the Gods love me , we are well entertained in being feasted in this place . I will that each person that passes by shall be invited to join the banquet .

sections.label: Section 5.4.686b
sections.translation: Agreed , so long only as , i ’ faith , each man comes with his own wine [*] ; for of this , a mouthful shall be given to no person but ourselves , this day . Eating alone [*] , let ’ s wait upon ourselves .

sections.label: Section 5.4.689b
sections.translation: This banquet ,

sections.label: Section 5.4.690
sections.original: pro opibus nostris satis commodule nucibus , fabulis , ficulis ,
sections.translation: for our means , is quite sufficient , with its nuts , beans , figs [*] , a dish of olives , pounded lupines , and a cake .

sections.label: Section 5.4.692
sections.original: Sat est : servo homini módeste melius facere sumptum quam ampliter .
sections.translation: It better becomes a man who is a slave to bring his expenses within moderation than beyond . Each one to his own station ; they , who have wealth at home , drink from cups , goblets , and bowls ; we , if we are now

sections.label: Section 5.4.695
sections.original: tamen bibimús nos , tamen efficimus pro opibus nostra moenia .
sections.translation: drinking from our Samian jug [*] , still build our walls according to our means .

sections.label: Section 5.4.696
sections.translation: On which side is each of us to recline by our mistress ?
